channel.title: Dialogue on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Dialogue on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Topic vs. Branched Based Dialogs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about topic versus branchb-based dialogue in RPGs.

This is a question that came from Simon Dah 3196, who asked: “What are the key differences between a topic-based dialogue system and a branching kind of system, especially if the goal is depth, with game state conditions and speech attributes or something along those lines — lines? This is a good question, Not only because it’s a good question, It’s something I’ I’ve never talked about, but also I’ve never even thought about talking about this before. And I should start by saying I’ve only done a topicbased dialogue system one time, and that was in Fallout, and it was hybridized with a branching system. So if you played the first Fallout, you know that there was a branch. When you clicked on NBC up popped a dialogue, a branching dialogue. But over to the side you was tell me about or ask about, there was a little box and you could type in whatever topic you wanted. So it was kind of topic based, but we didn’t display the topics to You, as you learn things in the game, you could ask NPCs about it. You could ask them about Brahmen, you could ask them about mutants, you could ask them about ghouls. So it was one of these things that as the player went along, they could just bring up different topics to NPCs to see if any one of them said something new or different, and it led to anything. And this was our way of trying to kind of hide everything to the from the player and not obviously displaying it. But you had to guess a keyword to try to open up something.

item.title: Personality Roleplay
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about personality roleplay.

This is the answer to a question from a channel member. Interestingly, they asked the question and I wanted to think about it because I wanted to make sure I answered it correctly, and also since I don’t write dialogues much anymore, I wanted to make sure I’d framed my answer correctly. Anyway, vasenkov asked: lately I’ve been thinking a lot, like a lot, about something I call personality roleplay. Perhaps designers have their own term for it. I’m talking about conversations with multiple reply options that ultimately or immediately lead to the same outcome and are basically just different flavors of the same reply.

item.title: Writing Evil Characters
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about writing evil characters.

Specifically, I want to answer a question, a second question, uh, from Nash kruka, who asks: how, where do you find yourself wanting to draw the line for evil options in an RPG? Similarly to how I find myself having a hard time choosing the evil option in a game, I find myself having a hard time writing them? Most games I’ve played have far fewer Bad Karma options than good. Maybe one evil dialogue to the half dozen good dialogue options. How do you weigh in on this? Okay, Nash, I know you’re asking about how to write dialogue for the player where it’s evil. Um, the way I think about this is: how would I write a good evil NPC?

item.title: Writing Good NPCs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about writing good NPCs.

Now I’m going to start with the big caveat that I’ve said on here many times: I am not a good writer. I’ve tried to be good, I’ve worked on It’s something I can improve past a certain limited amount of. I can write lore text. I can write short narration, but personal one-on-one dialogue — no, I’m not that good at, but I am good at seeing good writing of that category.

item.title: Social Conflict Resolution
item.description:
Hey everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about social resolutions in RPGs, and by that I mean combat resolutions that are social, not combat conflict resolutions that are social, it’s hard to talk about.

I’m going to answer a question from stavos Nicola, who’s answer, who’s asked several questions so far in this channel. He keeps asking good questions, so I’m going to keep answering them. And he asked asks: how do you feel about social, peaceful resolutions and RPGs? Many RPGs tend to use social persuasion based resolutions for Quest or challenges as a one roll skip combat Choice — rarely games and playy mini games or other forms of social combat that make these social encounters more interesting or even on par with the physical combat resolutions. Do you feel RPGs today sufficiently support the social pill pillar with engaging gameplay and, if not, how would you have liked it to be handled? So I think it’s pretty obvious that my answer is going to be that I like peaceful, social resolutions to conflict um. They’ve been part of every RPG I’ve made for the last 25 years.

item.title: Arcanum Dialogs
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I’m going to walk you through an Arcanum dialogue file explains how everything works and, along the way, kind of explain why we did it this way.

So I’m going to put the file up, boom. So this is a the actual example NPC dialog file that I made back when we were making Arcanum, to show people who would want to make dialogues for the game, kind of how it worked. So let me walk through how this works.

item.title: Arcanum Generated Dialog
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about arcanum’s generated dialogue system, and I know

I’ve been talking about Arcanum a lot, but I mean I’ve got the code right in front of me. I’ve been going through it a lot, I’ve been playing with it, and the generated dialogue system is something I’m particularly proud of because it let the few designers that were on arcanuman — we had even of the fewer designers, we had even fewer narrative designers — and it let those few people I mean think about. We only had 14 people Tops on Arcanum and just a few writing dialogue. They let them make so much content in the game, which so it was a good tool. But in particular, I liked it because it really doubled down on arcanum’s reactivity, and I’ll talk about why, um, when I get to the NPC one. But this could be really long, I’m probably talking really fast, but there’s just so much to talk about and it’s a detailed topic for me.

item.title: The Origin of Dumb Dialog
item.description:
Hi. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to tell a story.

One of my favorite stories. It’s the origin of dumb dialogue in Fallout, and not even just in Fallout, I mean.

Once we used it there, we did an Arcana we kept using. It most recently appeared in The Outer Worlds.

In fact, one of the narrative designers in a taipadar came to me one day and said: you know, dumb dialogue’s a lot of work, but whenever I get stuck I just think what would Tim say? I think he meant that as a compliment, anyway.
